1: Darius the Mede — one of the most debated figures in Daniel. No Median king of Babylon named Darius appears in extant cuneiform records; the city fell to Cyrus the Great of Persia in October 539 BCE. Proposed identifications include: (1) Gubaru, the Persian governor Cyrus appointed over Babylon, also known from tablets as "governor of Babylon and across the river"; (2) Cyaxares II, a figure in Xenophon's Cyropaedia not otherwise attested; (3) Cyrus himself, using a Median throne name. "Appointed king" (humlak, passive) — the passive construction in Daniel 9:1 (cf. Daniel 5:31) suggests someone elevated to a position rather than reigning by hereditary right, consistent with a governor or viceroy. The Median descent fits Gubaru's profile.
2: Jeremiah's 70 years — Daniel is reading Jeremiah 25:11-12 and Daniel 29:10, where YHWH promised 70 years of Babylonian dominion before restoration. With Babylon's fall in 539 BCE and Jeremiah's prophecy delivered ~605 BCE, the period was approaching its close. This is a remarkable scene: the prophet who receives new visions is also a careful reader of earlier Scripture, interpreting the present moment against the prior word of God. The act of discerning the "time of the end" through Scripture reading is a model of prophetic hermeneutics.
3: The penitential posture — sackcloth and ashes — was the standard mourning and supplication practice across the ANE. Sackcloth (saq) was coarse goat or camel hair cloth worn against the skin; ash (epher) was smeared on the face or sat in as a sign of grief and worthlessness. Fasting, sackcloth, and ashes together appear in Jonah 3:6 (Nineveh's repentance), Esther 4:1-3, and Nehemiah 9:1 — each time as communal crisis response. Daniel turns his face (panav) toward "the Lord God" — a directional act matching the temple-oriented prayer of 1 Kings 8:35, 48.
4: The prayer opens with the covenant-keeping formula: "great and awesome God, who keeps covenant and steadfast love (chesed) for those who love him and keep his commandments" — a direct echo of Deuteronomy 7:9 and Nehemiah 9:32. This is the berakhah (blessing/address) form standard in Second Temple Jewish prayer. The appeal is grounded not in Israel's merit but in YHWH's prior self-commitment (hesed) to the covenant — a standard feature of biblical lament and confession prayers (Ezra 9:6-15; Neh 9).
5: The fivefold confession — "we have sinned (chata'nu), acted wickedly (avinu), done evil (hirsha'nu), rebelled (maradnu), and turned aside (sarnu)" — exhausts Hebrew vocabulary for covenant violation. The accumulation is intentional: each term covers a different register of the transgression, from moral failure to willful rebellion. This confessional stacking pattern appears also in Solomon's temple prayer (1 Kings 8:47) and Leviticus 26:40.
6: The prophetic office in its social range: YHWH's prophets spoke to "our kings, our princes, our ancestors, and all the people of the land" — a complete vertical cross-section of Israelite society. The point is that no group can claim ignorance; the message was delivered at every level of the social hierarchy. The failure was not lack of information but refusal to hear — lo shamanu (we did not listen/obey).
7: "To you, O Lord, belongs righteousness (tsedaqah)" while "to us belongs shame (boshet panim)" — the rhetorical contrast of divine righteousness and human shame structures the entire prayer. "Open shame" (boshet panim, lit. "shame of face") is public disgrace in an honor-shame culture; the exile was experienced as a cosmic humiliation — proof of YHWH's rejection. The prayer reframes this: the shame belongs to Israel, not to God; YHWH's righteousness is vindicated by the very disaster that Israel interpreted as God's absence.
8: The shame of exile extends to every social stratum — "kings, princes, and ancestors" — emphasizing that the covenant failure was not one generation's but the cumulative defection of Israel's entire governing and priestly class. The prayer is a liturgical act of taking corporate responsibility before YHWH rather than deflecting blame onto circumstances or enemies.
9: "Mercy and forgiveness (rachamim u-selichot)" — the two divine attributes appealed to in contrast with Israel's rebellion. Selichot (plural of selichah) is the Jewish liturgical term for forgiveness prayers; the plural form implies abundance of forgiveness available. This is the sole note of hope in the litany of confession: the very covenant God whose commandments were broken is the God characterized by abundant forgiveness (Psalms 130:4, 7-8).
10: The formula "we have not obeyed the voice (qol) of YHWH our God" echoes Deuteronomy 8:20; Daniel 28:1-2, 15 — the covenantal listen-obey motif (shama' be-qol) that structures Deuteronomy throughout. The laws given "through his servants the prophets" frames the prophetic office as a communication of the Mosaic covenant to successive generations, not as innovation.
11: The Deuteronomic curse — "the curse and sworn judgment written in the Torah of Moses" (Deut 27-28, Daniel 30:1-10; Leviticus 26:14-45). Deuteronomy 28 specifically predicts exile, siege, starvation, and dispersal among the nations as consequences of covenant breaking. Daniel interprets the Babylonian exile explicitly as the fulfillment of Deuteronomic covenant curses — not random political catastrophe but precisely the covenant sanctions YHWH warned of through Moses.
12: "No disaster like what was done to Jerusalem" — the comparative superlative formula expressing incomparable catastrophe (cf. Joel 2:2; Daniel 12:1). The destruction of Jerusalem and the temple in 587/6 BCE by Nebuchadnezzar included the death of tens of thousands, the burning of the temple (which had stood for four centuries), the deportation of the leadership class, and the termination of Davidic monarchy. From a covenantal standpoint, it was the collapse of every institutional expression of God's covenant with Israel.
13: "We have not sought the favor of YHWH (challot et-penei YHWH)" — the idiom literally means "to make smooth/sweet the face of YHWH," i.e., to seek divine favor through prayer and righteous action. Even after the disaster, Israel did not turn to repentance. The verse is an indictment of the exile community itself: being in exile did not automatically produce the repentance that would restore the covenant relationship.
14: YHWH "watched for the right moment to bring the disaster" (shaqqad YHWH al-ha-ra'ah) — the same verb (shaqad, to be awake/watching) used in Jeremiah 1:12 for God watching over his word to perform it. Far from being absent or defeated, YHWH was actively providential in the catastrophe; the exile was not the triumph of Babylon's gods but the judicial act of Israel's own God executing covenant sanction.
15: The Exodus invocation — "who brought your people out of Egypt with a mighty hand and won for yourself renown" — grounds the prayer in Israel's foundational redemptive experience. The argument is: you delivered once, therefore deliver again. The Exodus was the paradigm event that established YHWH's identity as Israel's redeemer; every subsequent prayer for national deliverance appeals to it (Pss 77, 78, 105-106; Nehemiah 9:9-11).
16: Jerusalem is identified as "your city" and the surrounding land as "your holy mountain" — the covenant geography of divine ownership. The prayer appeals to YHWH's reputation among the nations: the city's desolation has made Israel "a reproach (cherpah) to all around us," which reflects back on YHWH's name. This is the classic Mosaic intercession motif (Numbers 14:13-16; Exodus 32:11-12): spare Israel for the sake of your own name among the nations.
17: The prayer shifts to petitions: "hear," "let your face shine (ha'er panekha) on your desolate sanctuary." The "shining face" language is from the Aaronic benediction (Numbers 6:25): YHWH's face shining on the sanctuary would signal restored favor and divine presence returning to the empty temple. The sanctuary is destroyed; what Daniel prays for is not merely its reconstruction but the return of YHWH's presence to dwell there again.
18: "Not because of our righteous deeds but because of your great mercy (rachamim)" — the prayer explicitly disclaims merit-based pleading. The appeal is entirely to divine character, not human achievement. This is the theological position of Ezra 9:13-15 and Nehemiah 9:33 as well: the restoration, when it comes, will demonstrate YHWH's grace, not Israel's worthiness.
19: The triple imperative — "O Lord, hear! O Lord, forgive! O Lord, listen and act!" — is the liturgical climax of the prayer. The urgency is expressed by the double call to listening (shema' / haqshivah) and the explicit "do not delay." The final motivation: "for your own sake, O my God" — the restoration is not only for Israel's benefit but for YHWH's own honor.
20: The phrase "while I was still speaking" — Gabriel's arrival is immediate, before the prayer concludes. The narrative detail that Daniel was confessing "the sin of my people Israel" and presenting the plea "before YHWH my God, for his holy mountain" establishes that the prayer was genuinely intercession, not merely personal petition. The response comes during prayer — a pattern also in Isaiah 65:24 ("before they call I will answer").
21: Gabriel — one of only two angels named in the canonical OT (the other being Michael). In Second Temple Jewish angelology (1 Enoch, Tobit), Gabriel was one of four archangels. His earlier appearance to Daniel was in Daniel 8 (the vision of the ram and goat). Yu'aph bi-yephia' — translated "swift flight" or "caused to fly in exhaustion/weariness"; the Aramaic is ambiguous. The "evening sacrifice" (minchah ha-erev) was offered at approximately 3 PM — giving a time anchor for the revelation.
22: Gabriel's explicit purpose is "to give you insight (haskel) and understanding (binah)" — the same epistemic vocabulary of Daniel 1. The angel's function is not to replace Daniel's study of Scripture (Daniel 9:2) but to illuminate what his study of Jeremiah had opened up. Revelation builds on and interprets prior Scripture rather than superseding it.
23: Highly esteemed / greatly beloved — chamudot (lit. "precious things" / "delights"), translated variously as "greatly beloved," "highly esteemed," or "treasure." The term appears again in 10:11 and Daniel 10:19, becoming Daniel's characteristic divine epithet. It implies both personal affection and covenantal standing before God. "A word went out" at the beginning of his prayer — the phrase stresses instantaneous divine responsiveness, undermining any sense that prayer must overcome divine reluctance.
24: Seventy weeks (shivim shavuim, 70 sevens = 490 years) — the heptad structure extends Jeremiah's 70 years into a sabbatical framework drawn from the Jubilee calendar (Leviticus 25:8). Six purposes are listed for the 70 weeks: (1) to finish/restrain the transgression; (2) to put an end to sins; (3) to atone for iniquity; (4) to bring in everlasting righteousness; (5) to seal up vision and prophecy; (6) to anoint a most holy place/one. The interpretive schools differ significantly: the historical school sees fulfillment in the Maccabean period (Antiochus IV and the rededication of 164 BCE); the prophetic school reads it as pointing to Christ's first and second coming; the symbolic school treats it as non-calendar theological statement; combined approaches find both a near and far fulfillment.
25: The decree to restore and rebuild Jerusalem — four candidates are debated: (1) Cyrus's decree, 538 BCE (Ezra 1:1-4); (2) Darius I's confirmation decree, 519 BCE (Ezra 6:1-12); (3) Artaxerxes I's decree to Ezra, 457 BCE (Ezra 7:12-26); (4) Artaxerxes I's authorization to Nehemiah, 445 BCE (Nehemiah 2:1-8). Working from 445 BCE with prophetic years of 360 days, 69 weeks (483 years) brings one to approximately 32-33 CE — a calculation made famous by Sir Robert Anderson in The Coming Prince (1895). The phrase "Anointed One, the prince (mashiach nagid)" — both terms are royal/priestly titles; mashiach simply means "anointed one" and does not by itself specify messianic fulfillment in the later theological sense.
26: Cut off and have nothing (yikaret ve-eyn lo) — "cut off" is the covenantal excision term (Leviticus 7:20-21), but here applied to the Anointed One himself. The passive construction ("have nothing" or "have no one for him") may indicate either rejection or sacrificial death without a following generation. The people of the prince to come destroying city and sanctuary — if "the prince" of Daniel 9:26 differs from "the Anointed One" of Daniel 9:25, then this refers to a future destructive ruler whose people (army) destroys Jerusalem: this is traditionally identified with the Roman destruction of 70 CE (Titus). The "flood" (shiteph) metaphor for irresistible military conquest appears also in Isaiah 8:8 and Nahum 1:8.
27: The final week: the "he" who makes a covenant with many is grammatically ambiguous — either the "prince who is to come" (Daniel 9:26) or possibly the Anointed One. The "middle of the week" cessation of sacrifice and the "abomination that desolates" (shiqquts meshomem) — this phrase is cited directly by Jesus (Matthew 24:15) as pointing to a future event, suggesting that whatever its initial fulfillment, it retains future referent. In the Maccabean reading, Antiochus IV's altar to Zeus in 167 BCE fulfills this; in the prophetic reading, it points to a future eschatological desecration. The "appointed end (kalah ve-necheratsat) will be poured out on the desolator" — whatever the agent, its termination is divinely decreed.
